FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=10,246, baseline ages 9-10 years, data from ABCD 4.0 release (2016-2020)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying social media use (hours per day on Facebook, Instagram, Twitter) measured at baseline via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey; total screen time across modalities (TV, videos, video games, texting, video chatting, social media). Applies B1 - separate coefficients for SM and total screen time reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses with odds ratios (ORs) computed for prospective associations between baseline screen time and year-two eating disorder symptoms; cross-sectional logistic regression for problematic screen use associations

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals reported in Tables 2 and 3 for all associations; prospective ORs range 1.05-1.55, cross-sectional ORs range 1.26-1.82; p-values reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis from U.S. ABCD Study cohort; prospective design with baseline exposure (screen time year 0) and year-two outcome assessment (eating disorder symptoms)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research article (not preprint, duplicate, or deposit); no evidence of prior publication

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone/SM is the independent variable predicting eating disorder symptom outcomes (not the dependent variable). Does not meet condition (a) - paper contains SM→outcome analyses, disqualifying FT-EC7 applicability per B18.
notes: Large prospective U.S. cohort study with multiple screen time modalities assessed separately and combined. Both total screen time and social media use show prospective associations with eating disorder symptoms at two-year follow-up. Problematic screen use measures (SMAQ, MPIQ) assessed cross-sectionally at year two. Sample weighted to U.S. demographic distribution.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, n=8,489 analytic sample, baseline ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time included as covariate in models but reported only in aggregate descriptive statistics (mean 416.35 minutes per week). No separate regression coefficient or effect estimate provided for screen time as a predictor. Applies B10 - SM/screen time is covariate-only with no coefficient reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple logistic and linear regressions used to examine pet ownership associations with family involvement in sports, physical activity, and screen time; regression coefficients reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Tables 2-3 report coefficients (ORs, β, 95% CIs, p-values) for pet ownership and outcomes (physical activity, sports involvement, screen time), but screen time values not clearly extracted as separate numeric results

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis from U.S. ABCD Study; longitudinal design examining associations between pet ownership and family involvement in activities

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article (2024, Anthrozoos); no evidence of duplication

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT-IC2 (no qualifying smartphone/SM exposure with its own extracted coefficient); screen time is controlled but not analyzed as primary exposure
notes: Study focused on pet ownership as primary predictor of physical activity and sports involvement. Screen time measured via Parent Screentime Questionnaire and Youth Screen Time survey, included in models but not as primary exposure of interest. Results indicate no meaningful relationship between pet type and screen time after adjusting for covariates.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,876 baseline cohort assessed ages 9-12 years across three waves of data collection

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying screen usage types measured: social media, texting, video chat, TV, online video/YouTube, video games. Multiple modalities assessed separately via Screen Time Survey; reported as distinct nodes in network analysis. Applies B1 - separate analytic treatment of SM, texting, video chat, and other screen types with individual edge weights reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal network analysis models estimating connections between screen usage types and psychotic-like experience (PLE) symptoms across three time points (ages 9-10, 10-11, 11-12). Network edge weights computed as effect size measures; cross-sectional network centrality indices calculated

FT_IC4.evidence: Network edge weights (r values ranging 0-0.362 across time points) reported in Tables 2-4; centrality measures (expected influence, betweenness, closeness) with numeric values provided; t-values reported for network comparison tests; p-values reported for network stability differences

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis from U.S. ABCD Study; longitudinal network analysis of screen usage patterns and PLEs across ages 9-12

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article (Comprehensive Psychiatry, 2024); no evidence of duplication

FT_EC7_reason: Screen usage types (social media, texting, video chat, etc.) are independent variables predicting PLE outcomes. Paper contains screen→PLE analyses, disqualifying FT-EC7 per condition (a) - SM/screen is not dependent variable in every analysis.
notes: Longitudinal network analysis examining clustering of screen usage types and associations with PLEs. Two clusters identified: digital media for socializing (social media/texting/video chat) and digital media for entertainment (online video/video games/TV). Texting and online video had highest centrality. Network structure significantly different between ages 9-10 and 10-12 years. Applies B1 - separate per-item screen type treatment in network.
